revision.comment: initial text of a stub article -- 1st steps in a constructive direction
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]].
The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list. In 1994, Ichijō augmented the list further — including Umenomiya.<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
== See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Umenomiya Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]]
{{Shinto-stub}} {{Kyoto-geo-stub}}
[[ja:梅宮神社]]

{{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]].
The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 1994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and {{nihongo|Gion Shrine|梅宮神社|''Gion-sha''}}, which is now known as {{nihongo|[[Yasaka Jinja]]|八坂神社|''Yasaka jinja''}}.<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} | image = Umemiya torii.jpg | caption = Red [[torii]] at Umenomiya Shrine. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|15|N|135|41|42|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 965 | date_destroyed = | location = [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Umenomiya Taisha romon.jpg|250px|thumb|Main gate of Umenomiya Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and [[Yasaka Shrine|Gion Shrine]], which is now known as [[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Jinja]].<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Umenomiya Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
== See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== * Umenomiya Shrine: [http://www.umenomiya.or.jp Official web site] {{in lang|ja}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and [[Yasaka Shrine|Gion Shrine]], which is now known as [[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Jinja]].<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Umenomiya Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and [[Yasaka Shrine|Gion Shrine]], which is now known as [[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Jinja]].<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Umenomiya Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} | image = Umemiya torii.jpg | caption = Red [[torii]] at Umenomiya Shrine. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|15|N|135|41|42|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 965 | date_destroyed = | location = [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Umenomiya Taisha romon.jpg|250px|thumb|Main gate of Umenomiya Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, [[Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and [[Yasaka Shrine|Gion Shrine]], which is now known as [[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Jinja]].<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Umenomiya Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Umenomiya Taisha]][[en:Umenomiya Taisha]][[fr:Umenomiya-taisha]][[ja:梅宮大社]][[ru:Умэномия-тайся]][[zh:梅宮大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q276781]] Wikidata name: Umenomiya Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} | image = Umemiya torii.jpg | caption = Red [[torii]] at Umenomiya Shrine. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|15|N|135|41|42|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 965 | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Umenomiya Taisha romon.jpg|250px|thumb|Main gate of Umenomiya Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and [[Yasaka Shrine|Gion Shrine]], which is now known as [[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Jinja]].<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Umenomiya Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Umenomiya Taisha]][[en:Umenomiya Taisha]][[fr:Umenomiya-taisha]][[ja:梅宮大社]][[ru:Умэномия-тайся]][[zh:梅宮大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q276781]] Wikidata name: Umenomiya Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} | image = Umemiya torii.jpg | caption = Red [[torii]] at Umenomiya Shrine. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|15|N|135|41|42|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 965 | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Umenomiya Taisha romon.jpg|250px|thumb|Main gate of Umenomiya Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and [[Yasaka Shrine|Gion Shrine]], which is now known as [[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Jinja]].<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Umenomiya Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
== See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== * Umenomiya Shrine: [http://www.umenomiya.or.jp Official web site] {{in lang|ja}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Umenomiya Taisha]][[en:Umenomiya Taisha]][[fr:Umenomiya-taisha]][[ja:梅宮大社]][[ru:Умэномия-тайся]][[zh:梅宮大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Umenomiya Taisha]][[en:Umenomiya Taisha]][[fr:Umenomiya-taisha]][[ja:梅宮大社]][[ru:Умэномия-тайся]][[zh:梅宮大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Umenomiya Taisha]][[en:Umenomiya Taisha]][[fr:Umenomiya-taisha]][[ja:梅宮大社]][[ru:Умэномия-тайся]][[zh:梅宮大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q276781]] Wikidata name: Umenomiya Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} | image = Umemiya torii.jpg | caption = Red [[torii]] at Umenomiya Shrine. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|15|N|135|41|42|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 965 | date_destroyed = | location = [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Umenomiya Taisha romon.jpg|250px|thumb|Main gate of Umenomiya Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|Ukyō-ku]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]] added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and [[Yasaka Shrine|Gion Shrine]], which is now known as [[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Jinja]].<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Umenomiya Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
== See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
==External links== * Umenomiya Shrine: [http://www.umenomiya.or.jp Official web site] {{in lang|ja}}

==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and [[Yasaka Shrine|Gion Shrine]], which is now known as [[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Jinja]].<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Umenomiya Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} | image = Umemiya torii.jpg | caption = Red [[torii]] at Umenomiya Shrine. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|15|N|135|41|42|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 965 | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Umenomiya Taisha romon.jpg|250px|thumb|Main gate of Umenomiya Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and [[Yasaka Shrine|Gion Shrine]], which is now known as [[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Jinja]].<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Umenomiya Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>

(1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Umenomiya Taisha]][[en:Umenomiya Taisha]][[fr:Umenomiya-taisha]][[ja:梅宮大社]][[ru:Умэномия-тайся]][[zh:梅宮大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Umenomiya Taisha]][[en:Umenomiya Taisha]][[fr:Umenomiya-taisha]][[ja:梅宮大社]][[ru:Умэномия-тайся]][[zh:梅宮大社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Umenomiya Taisha]][[fr:Umenomiya-taisha]][[ja:梅宮大社]][[ru:Умэномия-тайся]][[zh:梅宮大社]]
{{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} | image = Umemiya torii.jpg | caption = Red [[torii]] at Umenomiya Shrine. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|15|N|135|41|42|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 965 | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Umenomiya Taisha romon.jpg|250px|thumb|Main gate of Umenomiya Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Umenomiya Shrine'''|梅宮大社|''Umenomiya taisha''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Ukyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q580186|lt=Ukyō-ku|1=Ukyō-ku, Kyoto}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref> and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding {{nihongo|Umenomiya Shrine|梅宮神社|''Umenomiya-jinja''}} and [[Yasaka Shrine|Gion Shrine]], which is now known as [[Yasaka Shrine|Yasaka Jinja]].<ref name="pf118">Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Umenomiya Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kanpei-chūsha'']]|官幣中社}}, meaning that it stood in the second Rank of government supported shrines.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126.</ref>
== See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]
